one_line_takeaway: Efficient Lipschitz verification (LipSDP/ECLipsE) is provably blind to reachability - an infinitesimal, poly-time-undetectable bias tweak can make a network's true Lipschitz constant arbitrarily large - so certified-robustness bounds on arbitrary nets are fundamentally untrustworthy; instead, cheaply regularize the trivial bound and use verifiable-by-construction trigonometric layers.
key_finding: The looseness of efficient Lipschitz verifiers is a STRUCTURAL consequence of ignoring reachability/biases, and it afflicts every instance, not just worst cases: an infinitesimal, undetectable bias perturbation can blow up a network's true Lipschitz constant, and any polynomial-time verifier (SDP included) is provably blind to it unless P=NP. Constructively, much of SDP's apparent accuracy is 'gauge' slack recoverable far more cheaply: on the MNIST repro, ECLipsE tightened the trivial bound from 26.78 to 17.88 (in 04:46), but a quasi-Newton search over diagonal reparameterizations reached 21.94 in 00:31 (~1/10 the time, >half of SDP's improvement). Directly penalizing the trivial bound during training tightens it dramatically (three-way MNIST K/bL from 12.60 to 1.27) at negligible accuracy cost, and the proposed bias-free cos/sin layer pushes K/bL to 1.21 while raising test accuracy to 0.9826 - supporting the thesis that verifiable-by-construction architectures beat post-hoc verification of arbitrary nets.
core_contribution: A theory-first, largely negative paper on verifying the global 2-norm Lipschitz constant of unconstrained feed-forward neural networks (the quantity that underpins certified adversarial robustness). Its OWN contributions are: (1) A reframing argument that Lipschitz verification is hard because it implicitly requires knowing which hidden states are REACHABLE, and layer reachability is NP-hard (reduces to SAT, since ReLU nets encode Boolean circuits). (2) A structural taxonomy of composition units (ASL, SLA, LAS vs. the universally-approximating LASL = linear-activation-shift-linear): Theorem 2.4 shows the cheap 'trivial bound' (product of layerwise spectral norms) is TIGHT for the shallow ASL/SLA/LAS slices, while Theorems 2.5-2.6 construct explicit LASL units where the trivial bound is Omega(d) loose for ReLU/tanh (polynomial per-layer conservatism, toggled purely by biases) and INFINITELY loose for sine (true constant 0 while the bound is Omega(d)). (3) A no-go theorem (Thm 2.7, 'per-instance indistinguishability') showing that for ANY ReLU net of >3 layers, one can add one neuron per two consecutive layers so the resulting net is either identical to the original OR has an arbitrarily large Lipschitz constant, with the two cases toggled by an infinitesimal bias perturbation and provably indistinguishable to any polynomial-time verifier unless P=NP. (4) A demonstration that SDP is neither sufficient nor necessary: Fact 2.3 shows the ECLipsE-Fast scalar variant degenerates to the trivial bound for the symmetric slope sector alpha=-beta of sin; Theorems 2.8-2.9 show 'gauge' looseness from ReLU homogeneity/ill-conditioning can be removed by cheap continuous optimization of a diagonal reparameterization, recovering (in a linear identity-net model) the exact constant when every weight matrix has condition number 1. (5) A positive proposal: a bias-free trigonometric layer x -> A cos(Wx) + B sin(Wx) that is universally approximating yet, by the Pythagorean identity, has a trivial bound off from the truth by at most sqrt(2)*kappa(W) (Thm 3.1 lower bound), combined with directly regularizing/penalizing the trivial bound during training (Lagrangian penalty lambda * product-of-spectral-norms).
threat_model: The paper is verifier-centric rather than deployment-centric, but it contains a genuine, paper-grounded adversarial construction. Certified-robustness pipelines certify a network by upper-bounding its Lipschitz constant (small constant => provable robustness radius against adversarial input perturbations). Theorem 2.7 and the 'dead neuron' device (Eq. 7) show an adversary who controls the network parameters can embed a hidden high-sensitivity pathway (activated only on reachable-but-hard-to-find inputs, or a saturated 'dead' neuron with huge weight alpha) that inflates the TRUE Lipschitz constant arbitrarily, while the modified network remains polynomial-time indistinguishable from a benign one to every efficient verifier (SDP included). This is essentially a certificate/verification-evasion result: no efficient verifier can be trusted to detect such a planted network, so a 'certified' robustness bound can be silently vacuous. The paper explicitly frames its examples as adversarially perturbed networks and stresses they are hard to detect. Note the authors themselves (Broader Impacts) deliberately DIVORCE the algorithmic result from downstream deployment, so this threat framing is intrinsic to certified robustness but not the paper's stated application focus.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: the contribution is a fundamental algorithmic NEGATIVE result, so they do not extensively validate against softly-contradicting literature claims, nor assess downstream impact on modern/large architectures or realistic tasks - they are not championing a new ML methodology. Reviewer-added: (a) experiments are tabletop (MNIST, Iris; hidden widths ~4-100; single GPU/CPU, no error bars in the headline tables) and several results hinge on deliberately adversarial initializations rather than naturally trained networks; (b) the practical claim that trivial-bound regularization reliably reaches tight, 'live' minima rests on folk wisdom about overparameterized optimization, which the authors leave to future work; (c) the cos/sin lower bound (Thm 3.1) requires W full row rank and degrades with kappa(W); (d) 'true' Lipschitz constants are never computed - tightness uses an empirical lower bound bL, so reported ratios are upper bounds on true tightness; (e) the P!=NP no-go is a non-uniform hardness statement about verifier existence, not a claim that typical trained networks actually contain such adversarial devices.

datasets_used: MNIST (784->100->100->10 ReLU MLP; used for the trivial-vs-ECLipsE repro, the adversarial dead-neuron experiment, and the three-way ReLU/cos-sin comparison)
Iris (4 features, 3 classes; 120/30 stratified split; 2-layer cos/sin MLP with hidden width 4, to validate the Theorem 3.1 lower bound)
quantitative_results: MNIST repro (own): trivial bound 26.78; ECLipsE 17.88 in 04:46 (mm:ss); gauge-optimized trivial bound 21.94 in 00:31 (~1/10 time, >half of SDP's improvement recovered)
Dead-neuron MNIST, no penalty (Table F.1): trivial 30060.89, ECLipsE 6755.76, empirical LB bL 19.59; ECLipsE/bL = 344.82x still loose, trivial/bL = 1534.34x
Dead-neuron MNIST, lambda=1e-6 penalty (Table F.1): trivial falls to 218.58, ECLipsE to 130.19, bL 14.74; ECLipsE/bL = 8.83x; test acc 97.58% -> 96.43%

Three-way MNIST (Table G.2): ReLU lambda=0 K=605.171, bL=48.014, K/bL=12.60; ReLU lambda=1e-2 K=3.314, bL=2.614, K/bL=1.27; Cos/Sin lambda=1e-2 K=3.741, bL=3.082, K/bL=1.21 (K computed in ~0.02-0.03s each)
Three-way MNIST test accuracy (Table G.1): ReLU 0.9754, regularized ReLU 0.9809, Cos/Sin 0.9826
Iris (Table D.2): lambda=0 K=179.21, guaranteed LB K/(sqrt2*kappa)=25.04, empirical bL=105.74, K/bL=1.69; lambda=1e-2 K=5.84, guaranteed LB 0.82, bL=5.84, K/bL=1.00; test acc 0.933 -> 0.967

Theoretical rates: LASL trivial bound Omega(d) loose for ReLU/tanh (true constant tunable O(1) to Omega(d) via biases); infinitely loose for sine (Thm 2.6: bound 2d vs true constant 0); Thm 3.1 guarantees trivial bound within factor sqrt(2)*kappa(W) of truth for the cos/sin layer
Cited (NOT the authors' own experiments) motivating looseness of the trivial bound vs SDP: ~10^2 (Zhang et al. 2018, Fig 3) up to >10^20 (Fazlyab et al. 2019, Fig 2b)
baselines_compared: Trivial bound = product of layerwise spectral norms (Virmaux & Scaman 2018) - the cheap baseline the paper rehabilitates
LipSDP (Fazlyab et al. 2019) - the seminal SDP certificate, critiqued (not the authors' method)
ECLipsE / ECLipsE-Fast (Xu & Sivaranjani 2024) - layerwise SDP decomposition, the SDP baseline actually run in experiments

Authors' own alternatives: quasi-Newton gauge (diagonal-reparameterization) optimization of the trivial bound; direct Lagrangian trivial-bound regularization; and the bias-free cos/sin (SinCosTwo) trigonometric layer
Empirical lower bound bL via secant-ratio maximization with L-BFGS(-B), 20 restarts (used as the tightness denominator)
